content:
The escalation policy had four levels. Every level pointed at the same out-of-office calendar block.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Look, at 40,000 requests per second you don’t want your escalation graph to be a circle.
Sunday 3:17 AM. Payment webhook processor stalled. Queue depth 847,000 messages. Consumer pods healthy. Nothing consuming.
PagerDuty page to primary on-call — answered in 4 minutes.
Primary escalates to “Payments Platform Level 2” at 3:24 — no ack.
Level 3 at 3:34 — no ack.
Level 4 at 3:44 — no ack.
3:52 AM. Primary texts Sarah directly. Sarah’s auto-reply: “On PTO through March 21. Contact payments-oncall@.”
Sarah was Levels 2, 3, and 4. Same user ID. Three escalation tiers. One vacation.
34 minutes before backup engineer manually joined from #payments-eng scrollback.
How escalation policies become loops
Policy written 2022. Payments team: 4 people. Sarah senior. Sarah on every level “until we hire.”
Hires came. Policy didn’t.
# pd escalation policy export — simplified
Level 1: oncall-primary (rotation — works)
Level 2: sarah@company.com
Level 3: sarah@company.com # "backup"
Level 4: sarah@company.com # "director escalation"PagerDuty dutifully paged Sarah three times while queue depth hit 1.2 million.
Sarah’s phone off. PTO rule suppresses nothing if you’re the only target at each tier.
The thirty-four minutes of queue growth
3:17 — alert: consumer lag > 500k
3:21 — primary identifies stuck partition lock in consumer startup
3:24 — needs L2 authority to restart consumer StatefulSet (change policy)
3:24–3:52 — escalation into void
3:53 — #payments-eng backup engineer wakes, restarts consumers
3:58 — queue draining, 1.4 million peak depth
kubectl rollout restart statefulset/webhook-consumer
kubectl rollout status statefulset/webhook-consumer --timeout=180s
# partition lock cleared on fresh pod schedulingFix knowledge: primary had it at 3:21. Authority + warm body: 3:53.
Rotation vs escalation — different jobs
Rotation: who’s awake this week.
Escalation: who has authority + expertise when rotation is stuck.
Same human on three escalation tiers is not escalation. It’s delay with extra pages.
Fixed policy:
→ Level 1: PagerDuty rotation (primary) → Level 2: PagerDuty rotation (secondary) — different human, same week → Level 3: payments-oncall@ Google group → 3 people, round-robin → Level 4: engineering manager on-call rotation (separate schedule)
No individual named more than once in chain.
PTO integration we finally wired
PagerDuty + Google Calendar sync:
→ OOO event > 24 hours → auto-override escalation targets → Fallback: group alias, not named user → Weekly report: “escalation policies with single-user tiers”
First audit: 7 policies with duplicate user IDs across levels. Payments was not unique. Just the one that fired at 3 AM Sunday.
→ 30 Production Incidents That Cost $10K+ — escalation voids show up where the fix was known and the authority was on a beach. Queue depth is the bill.
Sunday morning money
847,000 stalled webhooks → ~$19,400 in delayed capture events (finance reconciliation Monday).
Not SEV1 outage. Customers mostly unaware. Merchants saw delayed settlement notifications. 34 minutes of policy debt.
Escalation policy review: 2 hours quarterly. Cheaper than finance asking why Sunday looked like Monday.
→ Production Incident War Room — escalation trees, three roles, who gets paged when primary is alone. Authority must exist at Level 2, not vacation.
The change policy that required Sarah
Consumer StatefulSet restart required “Payments Platform L2 approval” per change management.
L2 was Sarah. Sarah on PTO.
Emergency break-glass: SEV2+ allows primary to restart with post-incident review. Didn’t exist Sunday. Exists Monday.
# break-glass audit log entry — post-Sunday
# actor: oncall-primary
# action: statefulset/webhook-consumer restart
# sev: 2
# approval: emergency policy §4.234 minutes waiting for Sarah would have been 3 minutes with break-glass.
Quarterly escalation audit checklist
Copy-paste. We run first Monday each quarter.

• Export all escalation policies — grep duplicate user IDs

• Verify each level has ≥ 2 reachable targets or group alias

• Cross-check PTO calendar next 30 days against Level 2–4

• Test page: fire synthetic alert to staging policy, measure ack time

• Confirm break-glass paths documented for SEV2+
Sunday would have failed steps 1, 2, and 5.
What Sarah said Monday
Sarah was not annoyed. Sarah was embarrassed the policy still had her name three times.
“I thought we fixed that when Jake joined.”
We thought too. Thinking isn’t auditing.
Look, this is what I hand new hires now: escalation policy is infrastructure. Review it when someone joins or leaves. Not at 3 AM when the queue hits 1.2 million.
Group aliases vs humans in escalation
payments-oncall@ resolves to 3 people. PagerDuty pages group, first ack wins, others auto-resolve.
Sunday test after fix: synthetic alert, Level 2 ack in 47 seconds by Jake — not Sarah, not void.
Cost of group alias: Google Groups admin access. Someone must remove departed employees weekly. Cheaper than 1.4 million queued webhooks.
The StatefulSet restart policy debt
Separate from escalation: change policy required L2 for StatefulSet restart. Emergency break-glass unblocked primary.
Same Sunday fix: break-glass audit reviewed Monday. Approved. Policy updated — SEV2+ primary can restart stateful workloads with ticket auto-created.
Authority and escalation must align. Sarah on PTO exposed both failures at once.
The 1.4 million message backlog — downstream effects
Webhook queue depth 1.4 million at peak. Consumers caught up by 6:17 AM — 2 hours 24 minutes after fix.
Downstream:
→ 847 merchants received delayed settlement notifications → 23 called support before 7 AM → Finance reconciliation flagged $19,400 in delayed captures for manual review → One merchant threatened chargeback over “missing” webhook — webhook wasn’t missing, was 3 hours late
None of that pages like 12% error rate. All of it costs money and trust.
Synthetic escalation test — first Monday of month
# pd-test-escalation.sh — fires to staging service, not prod
curl -X POST pagerduty.com/.../trigger \
-d '{"routing_key":"STAGING_KEY","event_action":"trigger"}'Measure: ack at each level, time to human at Level 2, time to authority for break-glass action.
March test before Sunday: Level 2 ack 52 seconds. Sunday reality: 28 minutes void. Policy and reality diverged because Sarah wasn’t on staging test rotation — she was on prod policy only.
Finance’s spreadsheet — the meeting nobody wants
Monday finance asked: “how much did Sunday cost?”
$19,400 delayed captures. 34 minutes escalation void. 2 hours 24 minutes catch-up processing. 0 SEV1 declaration.
If we’d declared SEV2 at 3:17, comms template to merchants at 3:22. Half the support calls avoided.
Escalation policy is revenue policy dressed as ops config.
PagerDuty policy-as-code — the export in git
Escalation policies now live in git. PR required to change. Reviewer checks: duplicate user IDs, single-human tiers, PTO calendar next 30 days.
# payments-escalation.yaml
levels:
- targets: [oncall-primary-rotation]
- targets: [oncall-secondary-rotation]
- targets: [payments-oncall-group]
- targets: [eng-manager-oncall-rotation]Sarah removed from Levels 2–4 in same PR that added Jake. Sunday would have been caught in review. 2022 policy never got a PR.
Break-glass audit trail — what Sunday produced
{
"actor": "oncall-primary",
"action": "statefulset/webhook-consumer restart",
"severity": 2,
"policy": "emergency-break-glass-4.2",
"approval": "post-incident-review",
"timestamp": "2026-03-16T03:53:12Z"
}Monday review: approved. No discipline. Policy worked as designed once it existed.
Sunday without break-glass: primary waits for Sarah. Queue grows. Finance spreadsheet gets worse.
Consumer partition lock — the actual technical fix
Stuck partition lock on consumer startup — known issue, documented in payments runbook page 47.
Primary knew fix at 3:21. Couldn’t execute without authority. Break-glass + Jake’s ack at 3:53.
Technical debt: partition lock bug tracked 6 months. Sunday was symptom. Escalation void was amplifier.
Two fixes shipped Monday: escalation policy + partition lock patch.
Sarah’s vacation — the calendar integration detail
PagerDuty OOO sync reads Google Calendar primary only. Sarah’s PTO was on Work calendar. Sync missed it.
Fix: PTO events must be on primary calendar or explicitly shared with pagerduty-sync@.
Tiny detail. 34 minutes Sunday. Calendar hygiene is escalation hygiene.
Webhook queue recovery — the numbers after
3:58 AM — queue draining 12k messages/second.
6:17 AM — depth zero. 2h 19m catch-up from fix.
Merchants with webhook idempotency: fine. Merchants without: duplicate processing risk. 14 manual reconciliations Monday.
Escalation void didn’t just delay fix. It delayed idempotency guidance to comms.

Escalation loops are interview bait until they’re your 3 AM — Froquiz has on-call scenarios where the next page target is on PTO; practice before Sunday. → Froquiz
